_meta.description: Mapping of finding types to MITRE ATT&CK techniques an attacker could use to exploit them. This is a threat framework, not a compliance framework — references show which attacker techniques the misconfiguration enables. Techniques are drawn from the Cloud IaaS platform sub-matrix.
_meta.mapping_rationale: MITRE ATT&CK catalogues attacker behaviour. Not every misconfiguration cleanly maps to a specific technique — some are defensive control weaknesses that don't directly enable an attacker action, and forcing a mapping in those cases produces misleading noise. Mappings below prioritise honesty over completeness.

audit_notes.S3_PUBLIC_VIA_ACL: T1530 (Data from Cloud Storage) is a direct match. An attacker downloading objects from a publicly-readable bucket is exactly what this technique describes.
audit_notes.S3_PUBLIC_ACCESS_BLOCK_DISABLED: Corrected from an earlier draft that mapped this to T1078.004 (Valid Accounts: Cloud Accounts). That mapping was wrong — T1078.004 is about compromised credentials, and PAB being off doesn't enable credential-based attacks. T1580 (Cloud Infrastructure Discovery) is a weaker but more honest fit: an attacker in reconnaissance can discover misconfigured buckets more easily when PAB is off. Mapping defensive control weaknesses to attacker techniques is inherently forced; documented here rather than papered over.
audit_notes.S3_ENCRYPTION_DISABLED: T1530 covers this by extension — data extracted from cloud storage is more valuable and usable if not encrypted at rest. This is a secondary consequence rather than a primary enablement, but it is defensible.
